title: Regulatory Limits to Risk Management
abstract: Variable annuities, the largest liability of U.S. life insurers, are investment products containing long-dated minimum return guarantees. I show that guarantees with similar economic risks are treated differently by regulation and these differences impact insurers’ hedging behavior. When the regulatory regime recognizes certain risks, insurers start to hedge these risks in a substantial way. For some guarantees, this involves hedging both interest rate and equity market risks. However, for others, it involves hedging only equity market risk. As the regulatory regime still does not recognize the interest rate risk of all guarantees, insurers remain exposed to substantial interest rate risk.

article.1. Institutional Setting:

Variable annuities (VAs) are the largest liability of life insurers in the United States, with over $2 trillion outstanding as of 2018. These products, which combine mutual funds with long-dated minimum return guarantees, fulfill a growing demand for stable retirement income at a time when state pensions and defined-benefit plans have declined in importance. VAs are also a key source of risk for insurers, as the value of the guarantees provided to beneficiaries can rise substantially when equity markets fall or interest rates decline. For example, many U.S. insurers took significant losses on their VA liabilities during the 2008-2009 financial crisis, which prompted them to seek government bailouts, cut insurance supply, and engage in asset fire sales (Koijen and Yogo 2022;Ellul, Jotikasthira, and Lundblad 2011).1Given the size of insurers’ VA liabilities, the way insurers manage the risks embedded in them has significant implications for the insurance sector and the wider economy.

In this paper, I examine how the design of the regulatory framework impacts insurers’ risk management practices. There are two main challenges in studying how regulation affects risk management. First, detailed data on how financial institutions hedge risks and their usage of derivatives are typically unavailable. Insurers are unique because they report extremely detailed position-level data on derivatives, which makes it possible to comprehensively examine the determinants of their hedging behavior. Second, risk management decisions can be influenced by both economic and regulatory incentives. When we observe differences in hedging decisions across institutions, products or time, it is not immediately obvious whether economic or regulatory forces are driving this variation. I exploit a setting where regulatory incentives differ for products that carry very similar economic risks. This allows me to identify the extent to which hedging outcomes are shaped by regulation.

Two features of the setting are important. First, derivatives are the primary way by which insurers manage the risks associated with the guarantees embedded in VAs. The guarantees are essentially long-dated put options with complex payoffs and are exposed to both interest rate and equity market risks. These risks are far easier to hedge using derivatives than using securities because of the complexity of the payoffs, the structure of the cash flows, and the ease of dynamic trading with derivatives (Milliman 2021).

Second, while the regulatory regime for insurers has always recognized the risks of derivatives, essentially marking them to market, the way it has treated the risks of VA guarantees has evolved over time and in different ways for the different types of guarantees. Prior to 2009, the regulatory regime, for the most part, treated all VA guarantees as though they carried no interest rate risk and limited equity market risk. Thus, insurers using derivatives to hedge their VA guarantees would reduce the volatility of their economic capital but increase the volatility of their regulatory capital. In 2009, the regulatory framework was overhauled. For some VA guarantees, which I label “Risk Sensitive” (RS), the new regime recognized exposure to both interest rate and equity risks by incorporating greater risk sensitivities in how liabilities were computed. For other guarantees, which I label “Partially Risk Sensitive” (PRS), the new regime recognized only equity, but not interest rate risk.

I first show that RS and PRS guarantees have very similar underlying economic risks. Standard models of risk management (e.g.,Smith and Stulz 1985;Froot, Scharfstein, and Stein 1993) would imply then that insurers should hedge both types of guarantees in a similar way. In these models, economic capital is the binding constraint and, therefore, only the volatility of economic (not regulatory) capital is hedged. If the volatility of regulatory capital is costly, insurers could operate with greater capital ex ante and continue hedging their economic exposures (Froot and Stein 1998). However, if raising capital is costly and regulatory capital is the binding constraint, then hedging would differ across guarantees: interest rate risk hedging would diverge over time, increasing only for RS, but not for PRS, guarantees, but equity risk hedging would increase in a similar way for all guarantees.

I measure hedging by constructing a long history of risk exposures using holdings data on derivatives. Insurers report individual derivative positions, including all instruments (swaps, options, futures, and forwards) for each risk factor being hedged. Detailed contract characteristics are available for each position, including direction, maturity, and strike. Thus, as opposed to using the gross notional amounts, which are commonly used in the literature due to data limitations, I can measure hedging more accurately by aggregating up individual positions. The data start in 2004. This allows me to study how hedging has evolved under different regulatory regimes and market conditions.

Using a difference-in-differences strategy that exploits variation in regulatory hedging incentives over time and across products, I show that insurers’ hedging decisions are strongly impacted by regulatory incentives. I present two main results. First, insurers significantly increased interest rate hedging after the regulation is introduced: they hedged little before 2009, but hedging increases by more than five times by the end of the sample in 2016. Importantly, the increase in hedging is solely concentrated among insurers that are highly exposed to RS guarantees, but not to PRS guarantees. The differences in interest rate hedging for RS and PRS guarantees induced by the new regime are large. For example, for a 100-bps decline in interest rates, my estimates imply a hedging gap of 7.1 cents per dollar of capital for the average VA underwriter.2Second, in parallel results for equity hedging, I show that insurers also increased equity hedging after 2009, just like interest rate hedging. However, equity hedging increased in a similar way for both RS and PRS guarantees, such that the differences in hedging across guarantees induced by the new regime is negligible. This is consistent with their identical treatment under the new regulatory regime.

If we only observed variation in hedging over time or across coarse product groupings, it would be difficult to attribute the changes in hedging to regulation. For example, an increase in hedging over time does not by itself mean that regulation impacted hedging because hedging could have increased due to other reasons that may have coincided with the regulatory shift; for example, economic exposures may have changed or the financial crisis may have changed insurers’ risk appetite. Similarly, it would be difficult to conclude that regulation impacted hedging if I compared hedging across coarse product groupings as it is likely that risk exposures shift in a different manner for these products over time. The unique aspect of my setting is that I am able to compare the hedging for granular product groupings that have very similar economic exposures, but for which regulation changed differentially. In addition to variation over time and products, I also exploit variation across risk factors and find heterogeneity in which risk exposure an insurer hedges and which it does not. These choices line up with the incentives provided by regulation. Thus, making it unlikely that variation in economic incentives of hedging, for example, financial constraints or risk aversion, are driving these results.

A key question is if insurers hedge in other ways and regulation simply pushes them from one type of hedging to another. I provide three pieces of evidence suggesting this is not the case. First, reinsurance is another potential way to manage the risks of VAs (Koijen and Yogo 2022).3I, however, find no reduction in reinsurance after the regulation changes, which one would expect to see if insurers substitute out of reinsurance and into derivatives. In fact, as I discuss further below, insurers hedge a greater fraction of PRS guarantees with reinsurance after the regulation changes. Second, insurers can hedge the interest rate risk of VA guarantees by buying bonds. However, my analysis of insurers’ bond holdings reveals that insurers do not hedge VA guarantees with bonds. This is consistent with the fact that the interest rate risk embedded in VA guarantees is complex and would require complicated dynamic trading to hedge with bonds. Third, I quantify the interest rate exposures of VA guarantees for the insurance sector in the aggregate incorporating all risk management tools. My estimates suggest that the regulatory changes meaningfully closed the risk mismatch for RS: insurers hedge over 80%of RS exposures, largely through derivatives. However, a large fraction (⁠∼60%⁠) of PRS exposures remain unhedged.

Overall, my results show that insurers hedge the specific risks that the regulatory regime starts recognizing. An immediate implication is that because the regulatory regime does not yet recognize interest rate risk for PRS, insurers remain exposed to substantial interest rate risk. This is because PRS constitute around two-thirds of VA liabilities and the hedging gap between RS and PRS is large. This inference is consistent with the large sensitivities of insurers’ stock returns to interest rates that we see in the data. An important aspect of the new regime is also that it stops recognizing interest rate risk altogether when rates increase. This can further exacerbate insurers’ risk mismatch going forward.

A potential further implication is that prior to the regulatory changes insurers wanted to hedge, but the regulatory framework disincentivized hedging by not properly recognizing the risks of VAs. Prior to 2009, liabilities were largely insensitive, while derivatives were marked-to-market. Thus, insurers properly hedging their economic exposures would have increased the volatility of their regulatory capital. As the regulation changed, hedging incentives were restored, except for PRS guarantees where the regulation does not yet recognize interest rate risk. The evidence on reinsurance discussed above is consistent with this view. We observe insurers employing more reinsurance for PRS relative to RS, which suggests that they want to hedge PRS even though the regulatory regime does not fully incentivize hedging of PRS risks. More broadly, my findings suggest that deficiencies in the regulatory regime can lead financial institutions to bear more risk than they otherwise would.

This paper contributes to several broad strands of recent research on the hedging decisions of financial institutions. First, whether derivatives cause a build up of systemic risk or are used for risk management has been a central question since the financial crisis.Begenau, Piazzesi, and Schneider (2015)show that interest rate derivatives amplify balance sheet fluctuations for U.S. banks.Hoffmann et al. (2019)find the opposite for Euro area banks. This is the first paper to comprehensively measure the risk exposures of the derivatives portfolio of a large subset of the U.S. financial sector over a long period of time and across risk factors. Exploiting granular position-level data, which allows for accurate measurement of risk exposures, I show that insurers use derivatives to hedge the interest rate and equity risks embedded in VA guarantees.

Second, several papers document large heterogeneity in interest rate hedging across banks. The prominent explanations for this heterogeneity include product composition (Hoffmann et al. 2019), collateral constraints (Rampini, Viswanathan, and Vuillemey 2020), financial frictions (Vuillemey 2019), costs of distress (Purnanandam 2007), and low interest rate mismatch due to banks’ market power in deposit markets (Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl Forthcoming). To my knowledge, no other paper has examined how regulatory incentives impact risk management decisions and the use of derivatives. I find that differences in the regulatory treatment of products can lead to substantial heterogeneity in the hedging behavior across institutions. Moreover, the detailed data on derivatives allow me to shed light on several novel facets of hedging decisions, including the fraction of exposure hedged, how insurers use different instruments, and how hedging responds to changing macro-conditions.

My paper also contributes to the broader risk management literature on the motives of hedging.4In particular,Smith and Stulz (1985)andDeMarzo and Duffie (1992)propose that firms may care about the volatility in accounting exposures, instead of economic exposures if managers’ compensation contracts depend on accounting earnings. This may cause hedging to deviate from their economic benchmark. Consistent with these papers, I find that insurers target smoothing the volatility of regulatory capital as a risk management objective.

This paper also closely relates to the broader insurance literature on financial and regulatory frictions and their implications.5In particular,Koijen and Yogo (2022)examine the impact of financial constraints on the VA product market.Koijen and Yogo (2016)study the use of reinsurance as a tool to alleviate regulatory constraints.Ellul et al. (2020)document the impact of miscalibrated risk weights and hedging on asset allocation of VA underwriters. The results on insurers’ hedging decisions have consequences for product design, pricing, and market share of VA underwriters. My findings also shed light on why insurers were potentially less well hedged going into the crisis and why reinsurance activities increased, both of which can be traced back to the hedging incentives provided by the regulatory framework.
This section summarizes the main features of the institutional setting, namely, (a) VA guarantees have significant interest rate and equity exposures and are primarily hedged using derivatives. (b) There are four types of guarantees, and they carry similar economic risks. (c) The regulatory treatment of the guarantees shift in a different manner over time. This gives rise to different hedging incentives across guarantees and risk factors.

1.1 Variable annuities and hedging instruments

Variable annuities (VAs) are long-dated savings products sold by life insurance companies. VAs bundle together mutual funds with long-dated minimum return guarantees, that is, insurers guarantee contract holders a certain minimum rate of return, regardless of the actual performance of the underlying mutual fund. Thus, VAs can be thought of as insurers selling a long-dated put option (guarantees) on the mutual fund with complex payoffs: V A = Mutual Fund + Long-dated Put Option ⏟ G u a r a n t e e .

The guarantees expose life insurers to shifts in interest rates and equity markets. When equity markets or interest rates decline, the value of the embedded put option in VA goes up, leading to an increase in insurers’ liabilities. VA guarantees, therefore, pose significant risks for insurers, especially in adverse market conditions (Koijen and Yogo 2022).

Hedging instruments. Derivatives are the primary tool that insurers use to manage the risks associated with VA guarantees (Milliman 2021). While the guarantees could also potentially be hedged using securities, practitioners argue that securities are not used (see Section5). Another prominent tool to manage the risks of VAs is reinsurance (Koijen and Yogo 2022). The empirical tests, therefore, evaluate all potential hedging instruments, namely derivatives, reinsurance, and securities (bonds).

1.2 Types of VA guarantees and similarities in economic exposures

There are four types of VA guarantees. The guarantees are typically referred to as “GMxB,” that is, Guaranteed Minimum Benefit of type “x,” where “x” denotes (a) “A” (accumulation), (b) “D” (death), (c) “I” (income), and (d) “W” (withdrawal). I provide formal descriptions and numerical examples to illustrate how each guarantee works inAppendix A.

Similar economic risk exposures. The four types of guarantees carry similar economic risks. To quantify the risk exposures, I compute the value and the risk sensitivities of the different guarantees numerically as the risk exposures are not directly reported. I use a standard asset pricing model, where the short rate dynamics are given by the Cox-Ingersoll-Ross (1985) model and the equity market dynamics are as under the Black-Scholes model (seeAppendix A). For a realistic range of parameter specifications and under various interest rate and equity market conditions, which determine the extent to which the guarantees are in-the-money, I find that all the guarantee types have similar interest rate and equity risk sensitivities (Appendix Table A.2). Thus, shifts in interest rates and the equity market affect the economic value, and therefore the market-value based hedging requirements, of the different guarantee types in very similar ways.

1.3 Regulatory valuation framework and implications for risk management

Next, I describe the two features of the regulatory framework, (1) the treatment of liabilities and (2) the treatment of derivatives, that together give rise to differentregulatoryrisk management incentives across guarantees and over time.

1.3.1 Regulatory valuation of liabilities

Old regime. Traditionally, the valuation of regulatory liabilities occurred as though the guarantees carried no interest rate risk and limited equity market risk. The regime can be viewed as close to historical cost accounting. Liabilities were insensitive to shifts in interest rates and were discounted using the “valuation rate,” the prevailing interest rate at the time a contract is originated, and not current interest rates. Liabilities also, for the most part, did not reflect equity market movements, except to some extent large drops in equity market levels.6Thus, to a large extent, the value of regulatory liabilities did not change, from one valuation date to the next, despite changes in interest rates and equity markets.

New regime. A new regulatory framework known as Actuarial Guideline 43 was adopted in September 2008 and implemented in December 2009. The new regime recognized the guarantees’ exposures to both interest rate and equity risks by making the valuation of liabilities more risk sensitive relative to the old regime. However, this increase in sensitivities was not applied consistently across all guarantees and risk factors, as I describe below.

Under the new regime, regulatory liabilities are given by the maximum of liabilities computed using two alternative frameworks: Standard and Stochastic . Under the Stochastic framework, liabilities are computed by simulating the value of the VA guarantees for a range of interest rate and equity market paths, which depend on current market conditions, and then taking an average for the worst 30 % of the paths. In contrast, the Standard framework requires insurers to only stress the guarantees for a large instantaneous drop in equity markets but not changes in interest rates. Thus, the Stochastic framework is sensitive to both interest rate and equity risks, and the Standard framework is partially sensitive (only to equity risk): Regulatory Liabilities = max ( Standard, ⏟ Partially sensitive Stochastic ⏟ Sensitive )

Crucially, the relative importance of the two frameworks varies for the different guarantees. The Standard framework dominates for some of the guarantees (GMDB and GMIB) with a higher likelihood, while the Stochastic framework dominates for the other guarantees (GMAB and GMWB). Which framework ultimately binds depends on idiosyncratic contract features unrelated to interest rates, as I discuss inAppendix A. For example, the Standard framework is more stringent on mortality risk, which results it in dominating for one type of guarantee but not the others (Junus and Motiwalla 2009). Thus, after the new regulation took effect, the sensitivities of regulatory liabilities diverged for the different guarantees despite economic similarities. Some guarantees became sensitive tointerest rates, while others remained insensitive. In contrast,equity risksensitivities increased forallguarantees.7

To simplify the exposition, I henceforth label the VA guarantees where the Stochastic framework dominates as “Risk Sensitive” (RS), that is, sensitive to both interest rate and equity risk, and where the Standard framework dominates as “Partially Risk Sensitive” (PRS), that is, sensitive to equity, but not to interest rate risk.Figure 1summarizes the regulatory changes of VA guarantees over time. For completeness, it also depicts the treatment of non-VA liabilities, which continue to be valued under the old regulatory regime.

1.3.2 Regulatory valuation of derivatives

The regulatory regime has always recognized the risks of derivatives, essentially marking them to market. Thus, unlike liabilities, the value of derivatives fluctuates with movements in the underlying risk factors. The disconnect in the treatment of liabilities and derivatives arises because a vast majority of the derivatives (93%by gross notional values) do not qualify for the hedge accounting treatment, which, to a large extent, would have helped to resolve the different treatment that derivatives and liabilities receive under the regulatory regime. To qualify, derivatives must closely replicate the underlying liabilities. This turns out to be challenging as VAs are long-dated options and the market for traded long-dated derivatives tend to be thin and illiquid, as I discuss in detail inAppendix A. As a result, insurers using derivatives to properly hedge their economic risks would reduce the volatility of their economic capital but generally increase the volatility of their regulatory capital.8Thus, some insurers may have incentives to forgo hedging to the extent that they dislike the volatility of their regulatory capital.9

1.3.3 Implications for risk management

If insurers want to reduce the volatility of their regulatory capital, these features of the regulatory framework should give rise to different hedging outcomes across guarantee types, risk factors, and over time. In particular,interest ratehedging should increase for RS, but not PRS, guarantees after the regulatory changes, andequityhedging should increase for all guarantees. Instead if insurers do not care about the volatility of their regulatory capital, then hedging outcomes across guarantees would be similar as the guarantees have similar economic exposures.

Why might insurers dislike the volatility in regulatory capital and adjust hedging behavior?First, insurance capital regulation requires that insurers maintain a high risk-based capital (RBC) ratio, which is the ratio of the regulatory capital (assets minus liabilities) to required capital. If regulatory capital is too low, insurers may be forced to raise costly external financing. Thus, the presence of RBC constraints make insurers implicitly averse to the volatility in regulatory capital. Second, the volatility in regulatory capital causes fluctuations in the RBC ratio, which could result in more frequent regulatory exams and actions. Third, insurers’ credit ratings, which are an important determinant of future demand and product prices, are significantly influenced by the RBC ratio (Koijen and Yogo 2015a). To sustain a certain credit rating, insurers are required to maintain the RBC ratio above a threshold level. Volatility in the RBC ratio may make it more likely that credit ratings are downgraded.

2.1 Testable predictions

The shift in the regulatory framework resulted in an increase inψas some VA guarantees became more sensitive to interest rate changes. Moreover, due to the differences in the amount of RS guarantees underwritten, the shift in the regulation resulted in heterogeneity inψacross insurers. That is, insurers that have more RS guarantees have a higherψ⁠. This gives the following predictions, which I test using detailed contract level data on derivatives.

Prediction 1:Magnitude of hedging: Over time, an increase inψleads to an increase in hedging, all else being equal, as shown in implication 2. Moreover, for two firms,iandj⁠, all else being equal, ifψi>ψjthenδ^0i>δ^0j⁠. Thus, the impact of the shift in the regulation on hedging demand is higher for firms with a higherψ⁠.

Prediction 2:Dynamics between hedging and interest rates: An increase inψimplies a higher sensitivity of hedging demand to interest rates, all else being equal, as∂2δ^0∂ψ∂y0<0⁠. In other words, if interest rates fall, hedging demand increases, but the increase is higher whenψis higher. Moreover, for two firms,iandj⁠, all else being equal, ifψi>ψjthen∂δ^0i∂y0<∂δ^0j∂y0⁠. Thus, firms with a higherψ⁠, adjust hedging demand more in response to changes in interest rates, than firm with lowerψ⁠, all else being equal.

Prediction 3:Interaction with regulatory constraints: (A) As regulatory constraints become more binding (⁠λ(K^0)increases), the greater is the marginal effect of a shift inψas∂2δ^0∂λ(K^0)∂ψ>0⁠. Intuitively, when the regulatory constraints are more binding, the volatility of regulatory capital is more costly. If the regulatory framework is risk insensitive, this would lead to lower hedging because using more derivatives would have increased the volatility of regulatory capital. As the risk sensitivity of the regulatory framework increases, it would generate a larger increase in hedging for insurers that were ex ante more capital constrained as the risk insensitivity of the framework affected these insurers more. (B) Moreover, regulatory constraints affect hedging in opposite ways depending on the magnitude ofψ⁠. Whenψ=0⁠,∂δ^0∂λ(K^0)<0⁠. Thus, an increase in the importance of regulatory constraints leads to a decrease in the hedging demand. However, whenψ=1⁠,∂δ^0∂λ(K^0)>0⁠. Thus, an increase in the importance of regulatory constraints leads to an increase in hedging demand due to conservative regulationϕ⁠. I prove prediction 3 inAppendix B.
article.3 Data and Measurement:
I combine position-level data on interest rate and equity derivatives with data on VA guarantees, regulatory capital ratios, and other insurers’ characteristics to examine the impact of differences in regulatory treatment on hedging outcomes.

3.1 Derivatives holdings data

Life insurers report quarterly positions and transactions in derivatives, which I collect from the NAIC’s Schedule DB database. The data on individual derivatives positions are unique to the literature on risk management for several reasons. First, every single position for each life insurer is reported, including all the instruments held, across all the risk factors hedged, and over a long period of time, starting in 2004, which allows me to study the evolution of hedging over various interest rate, equity market, and regulatory regimes.16Second, detailed contract characteristics are available for each position, which allows me to compute the risk exposures of derivatives in a consistent way for each insurer and across multiple risk factors. Third, the data contain other important aspects of hedging at the position level, including a description of which balance sheet item is being hedged, how effective are the hedges (hedge accounting), and counterparties and collaterals posted. In aggregate, over the entire sample from 2004 to 2016, there are 2.6 million derivative positions, equaling over $60 trillion in notional value. The relevant data features are described in more detail below.

3.1.1 Risk factors

The major risk factors hedged are interest rate, equity, currency, and credit risk. For each position, starting in the year 2010, insurers report which risk factor is being hedged. However, before 2010, risk factors are not directly reported but can be discerned from the position descriptions, which I extract using textual analysis (seeAppendix E).Figure 2shows a breakdown of the gross notional values by risk factors. Interest rate derivatives are by far the largest, followed by equity derivatives. Moreover, interest rate and equity derivative holdings have been significantly increasing since 2009, when the regulatory regime changed, unlike the holdings of the other risk factors. I, henceforth, focus on interest rate and equity risks as these are the main financial risks that VA are exposed to.

3.1.2 Instruments and contract characteristics

The main derivative instruments include swaps, options, futures, and forwards. For interest rate risk, I restrict my analysis to swaps and options only, as these comprise more than 95%of all contracts by gross notional values.17For equity risk, I restrict attention to futures and put options as the bulk of these instruments are earmarked in the data as hedging VA guarantees. I only consider positions where the underlying is the S&P 500, which represent over 70%of all equity derivatives by gross notional amount.18

The main advantage of the data is that detailed contract characteristics are available for each position, including direction, maturity, strike, underlying, fair values, and trading data. Thus, as opposed to using the gross notional amounts, which may not reflect the true extent of hedging and the actual risk exposures, I can measure hedging more accurately by aggregating up the exposures of individual positions.19Some contract characteristics get reported in position description strings. I use textual analysis or the reported fair values to infer the relevant contract features in such cases (seeAppendix F). Overall, contract characteristics are available for 92%of the positions by gross notional values (seeAppendix Table F.2).

Table 1shows a snapshot of the data as of 2016:Q4. There is significant heterogeneity in the types of instruments for both interest rate and equity derivatives. For example, within the interest rate positions, 65%are swaps and 35%are options (caps, floors, and swaptions). Furthermore, there is also significant heterogeneity in contract features, including maturity and the direction of exposure. I convert each position into its corresponding risk exposures, fully accounting for the heterogeneity in the contract features (see Section4).

3.2 Which liabilities are derivatives hedging?

Next, I turn to the cross-sectional distribution of derivative holdings, which show that derivatives are predominantly concentrated in insurers that sell VA. First, to assess to what extent insurers use derivatives to hedge the different types of liabilities, I collect data on insurers’ liabilities from statutory filings. The main insurance liabilities are Life and Annuities. Annuities are further broken into Variable Annuities Guarantees (G), Fixed Annuities (FA), and Indexed Annuities (IA). I regress the gross notional amounts on the various liability types, controlling for other insurer characteristics (e.g., total assets and leverage).Appendix Table C.1shows a positive relationship between the size of derivative holdings and the amount of VA guarantees; that is, insurers that have more VA also hold more derivatives.20For example, as we move from the 25th to the 75th percentiles of the distribution of VA guarantees, gross notionals in interest rate derivatives go up more than 10 times from 7 cents to 73 cents per dollar of capital. Similarly, gross notionals in equity derivatives go up from 3 cents to 32 cents per dollar of capital. In contrast, there is negligible relationship between the size of derivative holdings and other types of liabilities, including life, FA, and IA.21

Second, consistent with the cross-sectional evidence, the aggregates also show that derivatives are primarily held by insurers that sell VA guarantees.Figure 3shows a breakdown of the gross notional amounts by whether or not insurers have VA guarantees as liabilities during a particular year. Insurers that have VA hold 98%of all derivatives on average across years. In other words, derivatives are concentrated in roughly the 25%of insurers that sell VA guarantees at some point. Overall, these facts imply that derivatives are primarily hedging the VA portion of insurers’ liabilities. Could insurers also use bonds to hedge the interest rate risk in VA? In Section5, I show that is not the case and, therefore, the main analysis focuses on derivatives. Next, I describe the VA data in greater detail.

3.3 Variable annuities data and measurement of regulatory risk sensitivities

Insurers report gross liabilities, amounts reinsured, and the account values for each VA guarantee at an annual frequency starting in the year 2005 in their statutory filings.22Gross liabilities measure the regulatory value of the guarantees underwritten, that is, the value of the embedded put option within a VA contract. Amounts reinsured are the liabilities ceded to another entity, which helps move risk exposures from the balance sheet of the insurer to a different entity. Account values measure the value of the mutual fund part of the VA contract. For each guarantee, I computenet liabilities, that is, the gross liabilities minus the amounts reinsured. Thus, the net liabilities measure the portion of the liabilities that still remain on the insurer’s balance sheet and affect the sensitivity of regulatory liabilities and capital.Table 2shows descriptive statistics of gross liabilities, reinsurance, and net liabilities for the different types of VA guarantees as of 2016:Q4.

The new regulatory framework resulted in a shift in the regulatory risk sensitivities of VA guarantees that have similar economic risk exposures. I measure this shift by the ex-ante ratio of VA guarantees to total capital and surplus (shortened to capital henceforth). I denote the ratio of total VA guarantees to capital by G i ⁠ . I split this into two parts: the ratio of risk sensitive guarantees to total capital, R S i ⁠ , and the ratio of partially risk sensitive guarantees to total capital, P R S i ⁠ . Specifically, I define: G i = (Net Liabilities in VA Guarantees) i , 2007 (Regulatory Capital) i , 2007 = (Net RS Liabilities + Net PRS Liabilities) i , 2007 (Regulatory Capital) i , 2007 = R S i + P R S i , (13) where the components of RS and PRS guarantees are defined in Section 1.3 . Table 2 shows the distributions of R S i and P R S i ⁠ . A higher R S i implies that regulatory capital became more sensitive to both interest rate and equity market movements after 2009. In contrast, a higher P R S i implies that regulatory capital became more sensitive to the equity market, but not to interest rate movements after 2009.

I define these ratios as of year-end 2007 for two reasons. First, I want to avoid the endogeneity concern that companies simultaneously choose their exposure in these products that are somehow correlated with the change in the regulation itself. Second, insurers report the VA liabilities after accounting for hedging. Thus, ex post it is unclear whether high liabilities are due to high prior exposures or low hedging. As the regulatory framework was adopted in 2008 and because insurers hedged little before 2008-2009 (see Section4), using liabilities as of 2007 helps to circumvent both issues. The identification strategy exploits the cross-insurer variation in the ex ante ratio of the different guarantee types to total capital (⁠RSiandPRSi⁠) to trace hedging across insurers, over time, and for each risk factor (interest rate and equity). The exact empirical specifications are discussed in Section4.

3.4 Insurers’ characteristics and sample

Regulatory constraints.The model shows that when the regulatory constraints are more binding, greater volatility of regulatory capital is more costly. To measure regulatory constraints, I collect data on the risk-based capital (RBC) ratio from insurers’ statutory filings, which are reported at an annual frequency. A high RBC ratio indicates that an insurer is well capitalized relative to its capital requirements.

Additional characteristics.The risk management literature provides several explanations for why hedging could vary across firms and over time. The main explanations include financing frictions (Froot, Scharfstein, and Stein 1993); ability to hedge and pay fixed hedging costs (Nance, Smith, and Smithson 1993); and net worth (collateral) constraints (Rampini and Viswanathan 2010). Measures of firm size and financing constraints proxy for these factors. I measure size using log(assets). Measures of financing constraints include leverage (general account assets divided by total capital), A.M. Best’s credit ratings, capital adequacy ratio (BCAR) ratio, and the guideline BCAR that sustains current ratings.

Sample.I aggregate balance sheets and derivative holdings at the insurance group level using NAIC company code to group code mappings. The sample includes insurers that held derivatives during the period 2004-2016. For example, 57 insurers held interest rate derivatives at some point during the sample period, which are predominantly insurers that sell VA guarantees (Figure 3). As I compare hedging before and after the shift in the regulatory framework, I only include insurers that have been in operation for at least 2 years in each regulatory regime. I also exclude insurers that have less than $100 million gross notional in rate derivatives combined across all quarters. The final sample contains 53 insurers, which account for 89.3%of the sector’s total assets at the end of 2016. I discuss the descriptive statistics and the main empirical findings in the next section.
article.4. Regulatory Incentives and Hedging wit:
4.1 Measurement of risk exposures of derivative holdings

I measure the extent of hedging by computing the risk exposures of the derivative holdings. Specifically, I compute the interest rate exposures for each position due to a 100-bps shift in the yield curve. I measure the risk exposures of swaps by dollar duration and of rate options by option “deltas” as implied by theBlack (1976)model. Thus, I assume parallel shifts of the yield curve, which as term structure decompositions show, account for a vast majority of the variation in yields.23I compute the equity risk exposures due to a 10%shift in the S&P 500 index. The exposures of futures are+1 for long and−1 for short positions for a $1 change in S&P 500, and for put options, I compute the Black-Scholes deltas.24

The measurement of risk exposures is a three-step process. First, I compute the exposures of a wide range of instruments that have different direction, maturity, and moneyness at the end of each quarter t given current interest rate and equity market conditions (e.g., zero and forward curves, the level of the S&P 500, and implied volatilities). 25 Second, I apply the exposures to the position-level data by matching the relevant contract features. For example, for swaps, I match the direction (receive or pay fixed), remaining maturity, and the swap rate, and for options, I match the strike, maturity, and the trade direction. Appendix F describes the methodology and the data steps. Finally, I compute the total dollar risk exposures δ i t for insurer i at time t for each risk factor by aggregating exposures across positions as follows: δ i t = ∑ j Δ j t × N i j t , (14) where Δ j t is the exposure of position j at time t ⁠ . 26 For each instrument, I compute exposures for a hypothetical $1 in notional value (interest rates) or a $1 change in the S&P 500 (equity). I then multiply exposures by N i j t ⁠ , which is the notional value in the case of interest rate positions or the number of contracts in the case of equity positions.

I aggregate the risk exposures across positions for the following reasons. First, some positions may have opposite directions and risk exposures (e.g., receive fixed vs. pay fixed swaps), aggregation allows the offsetting positions to net out. Moreover, positions that have higher exposures (e.g., long maturity swaps or in-the-money options) receive a greater weight in the aggregation relative to positions that have lower exposures. Thus,δitmeasures what would be the approximate totalnetchange in the value of the derivative holdings if interest rates were to shift by 100-bps or equity markets by 10%⁠.Figures 4and6show the time series of the total exposures from interest rate and equity derivatives, respectively, for the insurance sector as a whole.Appendix Fvalidates the accuracy of the exposure calculations by matching the joint dynamics of interest rate changes, calculated exposures, and the ex post reported changes in the value of insurers’ derivative holdings.

4.2 Interest rate risk hedging

4.2.1 Stylized facts

I start by documenting stylized facts on interest rate risk hedging. First, interest rate derivatives have apositiveduration on average, which implies that derivatives gain in value when interest rates decline (Table 3). Insurers are exposed to declines in interest rates because VA embed minimum return guarantees. As interest rates decline, these guarantees become more in-the-money and the value of the liabilities increase. A positive duration of derivatives, therefore, implies that insurers buy protection, that is, hedge declines in interest rates to offset the increase in liabilities. Consistent with this,Appendix Figure A.1shows that 96%of the positions are reported as being held for hedging and not speculation.

4.2.2 Impact of regulatory incentives

(a) Magnitude of hedging: Next, I formally examine the impact of the regulatory incentives on the evolution of interest rate risk hedging by estimating the specification below, which relates the shift in interest rate hedging across insurers after 2009, with the cross-sectional variation in the ratio of different types of VA guarantees to total capital, 28 δ i t ^ = α + ∑ J = R S , P R S β J ( J i × P o s t t ) + γ ′ X i t − 1 + α i + α t + ϵ i t , (15) where δ i t ^ is the total exposure from interest rate derivatives scaled by capital for insurer i at time t ⁠ . J = R S is the ratio of RS guarantees to capital and J = P R S is the ratio of PRS guarantees to capital, defined in Equation ( 13 ). P o s t t is a dummy variable that takes a value of one for years on or after 2009, and X i t − 1 are insurer-specific controls, discussed below. The preperiod is 2004 to 2008 and the postperiod is 2009 to 2016. I include insurer fixed effects, α i ⁠ , to control for non-time-varying unobservables across insurers and time fixed effects, α t ⁠ , to control for time trends, for example, changes in interest rate conditions that drive hedging demand, which absorb R S i ⁠ , P R S i ⁠ , and P o s t t ⁠ , respectively.

The coefficients of interest areβRSandβPRS⁠, which measure the correlation between the shift in interest rate risk hedging after 2009 and the exposure of insurers to RS and PRS guarantees, respectively. If insurers do not care about regulatory incentives, then interest rate hedging outcomes for both RS and PRS guarantees would be similar as the economic interest rate exposures are similar for both RS and PRS guarantees (seeAppendix Table A.2). However, if regulatory incentives matter, then hedging outcomes would be different because regulatory interest rate sensitivities are different for RS and PRS guarantees.

Table 5presents the results. I estimate a positive and statistically significant coefficient (at the 10%level; column 1) when I use the ratio of total VA guarantees to capital,Gi⁠, as the main regressor. Next, I split the variable into its constituents,RSiandPRSi⁠. I estimate a positive and statistically significant coefficientβRS⁠, which captures the interaction betweenRSiand the post 2009 dummy, at the 1%level. However,βPRSis statistically insignificant (column 2).29In columns 3 and 4, I introduce the two variables separately; however, the estimates ofβRSandβPRSdo not change materially.

Figure 5further corroborates the findings by describing the dynamics of the shift in hedging for the RS and PRS ratios for each year surrounding the introduction of the new regulation relative to a base year (2004). There is no change in hedging in the preperiod, but hedging increases substantially in the postperiod. Moreover, the increase in hedging correlates positively and significantly withRSi⁠, but not withPRSi⁠.

The differences in hedging between RS and PRS guarantees are large in magnitude after the regulatory shift. For theaverageinsurer that sells VA guarantees, my estimates imply a hedging gap (between RS and PRS guarantees) of 7.1 cents per dollar of capital for a 100-bps shift in interest rates after the regulatory shift, which can be interpreted as a duration gap of roughly 7 years. In other words, capital declines by 7 percentage points less due to a 100-bps decline in interest rates for RS, relative to PRS, guarantees.30

Differential shifts in economic exposures:A natural identification concern is whether economic risk exposures increase faster for high RS insurers than high PRS insurers when interest rates decline due to differences in convexity of exposures. If so, then the RS ratio measures not only the shift in the regulatory risk sensitivities, but also the shift in the economic risk exposures, which could explain the relative increase in interest rate risk hedging over time. However, asAppendix Ashows, PRS guarantees have quantitatively similar economic interest rate exposures as RS guarantees under different interest rates and equity market (moneyness) conditions. In other words, the shift in economic exposures, as a result of changes in interest rates and equity markets, is similar for both types of guarantees. Thus, the observed differences in hedging outcomes across guarantees after 2009 implies that insurers indeed care about the regulatory incentives.

To mitigate the concern about differential shifts in economic exposures further, I control for a number of variables that affect balance sheet convexities, which I label as convexity controls inTable 5. On the liability side, more generous guarantees imply a higher convexity because when interest rate are low fewer households surrender their contracts (Domanski, Shin, and Sushko 2017). I control for the generosity of guarantees by the ratio of the value of guarantees to total account value, as inKoijen and Yogo (2022). On the asset side, a higher proportion of mortgage-backed securities (MBS) or callable bonds could also imply a differential shift in exposures because of negative convexity (Hanson 2014), which implies an increase in exposures when rates fall. I control for the share of MBS and callable bonds in total assets. I also include the guarantee ratio quartile and MBS and callable bond share quartile×year fixed effects in columns 5 and 6. The estimates remain largely unaffected.

I control for a number of characteristics in addition to measures of economic risk exposures, for example, size (log assets), size quartile×year fixed effects, leverage, RBC ratio, credit rating, and BCAR, as will be discussed in detail below. Collectively, these findings are consistent withPrediction 1that the increase in the regulatory risk sensitivities would lead to an increase in hedging with derivatives for RS, but not PRS, guarantees.

(b) Dynamic hedging: As risk exposures increase when interest rates fall, an increase in hedging in response to a decline in interest rates reveals dynamic hedging of the underlying exposures. To examine the impact of the regulatory incentives on hedging dynamics, I estimate the specification below, which quantifies the relationship between changes in hedging and changes in interest rates in the cross-section and over time: Δ δ i t ^ = α + ∑ J = R S , P R S β D 1 J ( R 10 t × J i × P o s t t ) + ∑ J = R S , P R S β D 2 J ( R 10 t × J i ) + ∑ J = R S , P R S β D 3 J ( J i × P o s t t ) + γ ′ Δ X i t + α i + α t + ϵ i t , where Δ δ i t ^ is the quarterly change in hedging and R 10 t is the quarterly return on a 10-year Treasury bond from CRSP, which captures changes in interest rates. I include insurer fixed effects and time fixed effects, which absorb J i ⁠ , R 10 t × P o s t t ⁠ , R 10 t ⁠ , and P o s t t ⁠ . The main coefficient of interest is the loading on the triple interaction term β D 1 J ⁠ , which measures the correlation between the shift in the sensitivity of changes in hedging due to changes in interest rates and R S i and P R S i ⁠ .

Table 6shows that changes in hedging are sensitive to changes in interest rates (column 1). As interest rates decrease, and bond prices (thus, bond returns) rise, hedging increases. A 1%increase in bond returns leads to a 13.3 (bps) change in hedging exposure per dollar of capital. Moreover, this relationship is positively and significantly correlated with the RS ratio and uncorrelated with the PRS ratio (column 2). As the RS ratio increases, the covariation between changes in hedging and changes in interest rates also increases.

4.3 Equity risk hedging

4.3.1 Stylized facts

Next, I discuss the key stylized facts related to the hedging of equity risk of VA guarantees. First, the risk exposures of equity derivatives arenegative, that is, the equity derivatives gain in value when the stock market declines (Table 3). VA are exposed to a fall in the stock market because the embedded put option becomes in-the-money (and liabilities increase in value) when the stock market declines. A negative exposure of equity derivatives implies that, in turn, insurers have taken long positions in put options and short positions in equity futures, consistent with hedging the equity exposure in a VA guarantee.

Second,Figure 6shows that just as interest rate exposures increased, equity exposures have also increased substantially since 2009. A 10%shift in the S&P 500 would have resulted in a relatively small change in the value of the equity derivatives portfolio before 2009, however, the same shift would have led to a change of over $8 billion in 2016 for insurers in the aggregate. For example, there is a sixfold increase in risk exposures from equity derivatives for insurers in aggregate between 2008 and 2016. Similarly,Table 3, panel B shows that the average insurer that holds equity derivatives has had a large increase in equity exposures during the period 2009-2016 relative to the period 2004-2008.

4.3.2 Impact of regulatory incentives

(a) Magnitude of hedging:Next, I examine the impact of regulatory incentives on equity risk hedging. The examination of equity hedging outcomes is a useful test for the regulatory incentives hypothesis. Unlike interest rates, the regulatory equity risk sensitivities increased for all guarantees under the new regime as both the Standard and the Stochastic frameworks became more sensitive to shifts in the equity market (Figure 1). Moreover, the economic equity risk exposures are similar across guarantees (Appendix Table A.2). Thus, unlike interest rate hedging, the new regime should result in a similar increase in equity risk hedging for both RS and PRS guarantees. To test whether this is the case, I estimate the model in Equation (15) with equity hedging exposures as the dependent variable.

Table 7documents the main findings. I estimate a negative and statistically significant coefficient (at the 1%level) for the ratio of total VA guarantees to capital,Gi(column 1). The negative sign simply captures the negative exposures for equity derivatives, that is, long positions in put options and short positions in futures gain in value when the equity market declines. A larger negative coefficient implies anincreasein equity risk hedging. Next, I split the variable into its constituentsRSiandPRSi⁠. BothβRSandβPRSare statistically significant (columns 2-4). Thus, the shift in equity risk hedging after 2009 correlates not only with the RS but also with the PRS ratio.

4.4 Regulatory constraints and alternative explanations

4.4.1 Regulatory constraints

The model shows that an increase in the regulatory risk sensitivities would generate a larger increase in hedging for insurers that are constrained relative to those that are unconstrained prior to the regulatory changes (see Prediction 3). To evaluate this relationship between shift in hedging and regulatory constraints, I divide insurers into two groups by their RBC ratio in 2007 and reestimate Equation (15) for each group separately. Low (below-median) RBC are insurers that are more likely to be constrained and high (above-median) RBC are insurers that are more likely to be unconstrained.Table 8shows that the impact of the shift in the regulation on both interest rate and equity hedging is more pronounced for insurers that are constrained, consistent withPrediction 3(A).

Appendix Table C.2, column 1, shows that hedging the RBC ratio are positively (and statistically significantly) related. As the RBC ratio falls (constraints become tighter), hedging decreases. Moreover, the covariation between RBC and hedging changes over time and varies in the cross-section, depending on the RS ratio (columns 2-3). Low RS insurers (low regulatory sensitivity) hedge less when RBC declines. In contrast, high RS insurers (high regulatory sensitivity) hedge more when RBC declines, but only after 2009 when regulation shifts. The estimates of the full model in column 4 confirm these findings.βR1RSis negative and significant andβR2RSis positive and significant. Thus, the covariation between hedging and RBC changes sign after 2009, that is, insurers start to hedge more as they become more constrained. Moreover, the shift in the covariation is stronger for insurers who experienced a larger shift in the regulatory risk sensitivities. In addition,βR4RSis positive and statistically significant andβR3RSis insignificant. Thus, insurers with no regulatory interest rate risk sensitivity (⁠RSi=0⁠), hedge less as they become more constrained, and there is no shift in the covariation over time. Thus, consistent withPrediction 3(B), regulatory constraints affect hedging in opposite ways depending on the risk sensitivity of the framework.

4.4.2 Alternative explanations

My setting and empirical specifications address several alternative explanations. I first examine the extent to which the measures of the shift in regulatory sensitivities (⁠RSiandPRSi⁠) correlate with other firm characteristics that may also affect hedging.Appendix Table C.3shows that the RS and PRS ratios do not correlate with most characteristics, except insurers’ size. This is not surprising because my analysis conditions on insurers which hold derivatives and are, therefore, likely to be highly similar along most dimensions. However, differences in size could proxy for an insurer’s sophistication and ability to bear the fixed costs of hedging, which I address below along with other alternative explanations.

(a) Sophistication and fixed hedging costs.A number of tests help to rule out this explanation. First, all insurers hedge equity, but only a subset hedge interest rate risk. Sophistication and fixed hedging costs are unlikely to explain why some insurers hedge one risk, but not the other. Second, I examine how much insurers hedge (intensive margin) and not whether they hedge (extensive margin) as the estimations occur within an insurer controlling for insurer fixed effects. Moreover, my sample conditions on insurers that hold derivatives; thus, there is likely limited heterogeneity in firm’s sophistication across my sample of insurers. Third, I include the log of total assets (size) in all the specifications. I also include size quartile×year fixed effects. The estimated coefficients are robust to these control variables.

(b) Shifts in risk aversion and exposure to the financial crisis.Heterogeneity in risk aversions could lead to different hedging choices, even though the underlying economic exposures are the same. All specifications use insurer fixed effects, which help account for static differences in risk aversion across firms. However, risk aversions could have possibly shifted for a subset of insurers who were differently exposed to the financial crisis. In addition, it is also possible that insurers learned about the risks of VAs during the financial crisis, and this prompted more hedging. However, the results are unlikely to be driven by the differential exposure to (and learning from) the financial crisis.

First, if increase in risk aversion is driving the results, then it is unclear why insurers exposed to PRS guarantees do not increase interest rate hedging, but only increase equity hedging. Second, the empirical specifications control for the RBC ratio, leverage, and BCAR relative to the guideline, which proxy for financial constraints and the exposure to the financial crisis. Third, to explicitly account for the differential exposure to the financial crisis, I include the size of the shock experienced during the crisis, measured as changes in capital between 2007:Q4 and 2009:Q1, interacted with a postcrisis dummy inAppendix Table C.4(⁠Shocki×Postt⁠). If the changes in hedging are driven just by the increase in risk aversion of a subgroup of insurers that suffered a large shock during the crisis, thenShocki×Posttshould be statistically and economically significant and the loadings on RS and PRS ratios should be subsumed. However, the loadings on the RS and PRS ratios remain unaffected andShocki×Posttis insignificant, which imply that the increase in hedging is not just concentrated among insurers that were adversely affected during the crisis.

(c) Collateral constraints.A prominent explanation for the absence of hedging is collateral constraints (Rampini and Viswanathan 2010). In these models, firms’ future investments and risk management both compete for scarce capital. The key state variable is firms’ economic net worth and the prediction is that low net worth firms, because they have a greater opportunity cost, engage in less risk management. However, my central result cannot be explained by changing net worth of a certain set of insurers. It is unlikely that the regulatory change that I study perfectly maps to a certain subgroup of insurers which had their collateral constraints loosened in 2009. Having said that, the regressions control for the size of the shock experienced during the crisis, guarantee value, leverage, and RBC ratio, all of which proxy for financial constraints. Moreover, net worth constraints operate at the firm level. But I find heterogeneity in which risk exposures firms hedge and which they do not. These choices line up with the incentives provided by regulation.

Overall, the observed hedging behavior is consistent with the differential incentives provided by the regulatory framework, and not with cross-insurer differences in sophistication, fixed hedging costs, risk aversion, exposure to the crisis, or financial constraints.
article.5 Alternative Hedging Strategies:
In this section, I evaluate whether insurers hedge in other ways and regulation simply pushes them from one type of hedging to another. I evaluate two alternative hedging strategies: reinsurance and securities (bonds), which as discussed in Section1, are other possible ways to hedge VA guarantees.

5.1 Reinsurance

Using reinsurance, insurers can transfer risk exposures off-balance sheet, either to third-party reinsurers or to captives (also known as shadow insurers, which are special purpose vehicles and offshore domiciled entities within the same holding company). I evaluate two hypotheses related to the use of reinsurance as a risk management tool for VA guarantees.Hypothesis 1: Insurers shift risk management from reinsurance to derivatives after the regulation changes, but overall hedging remains the same.Hypothesis 2: Insurers highly exposed to PRS guarantees (that do not hedge interest rate risk using derivatives) should engage in more reinsurance if they want to hedge PRS risks using other ways. To evaluate these hypotheses, I exploit data on reinsurance at the guarantee level, which insurers report at an annual frequency in the statutory filings (see Section3).

5.1.1 Is there substitution from reinsurance to derivatives?

To evaluate whether there is substitution from reinsurance to derivatives after the regulatory changes, I ask whether reinsurance declines after 2009, particularly for RS guarantees for which hedging with derivatives increased substantially.Figure 7plots the proportion of liabilities reinsured for the two types of guarantees over time. Before 2009, on average about 25%to 30%of liabilities were reinsured for both guarantees. After 2009, there is no evidence to suggest that reinsurance declined for either RS or PRS guarantees. To formally evaluate the shifts in reinsurance behavior, I regress the proportion of liabilities reinsured on a post-2009 dummy variable.Table 9(panel A) documents no meaningful reduction in the use of reinsurance for either RS or PRS guarantees after 2009, suggesting that insurers do not substitute out of reinsurance and into derivatives.

5.1.2 Do insurers hedge PRS risks with reinsurance more?

Figure 7shows that relative to RS guarantees, a greater proportion of PRS guarantees are reinsured after the regulation shifts. Moreover,Table 9(panel A) documents that the average insurer increased reinsurance by 8 percentage points more for PRS guarantees than for RS guarantees after 2009. This constitutes an economically large (29%⁠) increase in PRS reinsurance for the average insurer.

Regulatory incentives.As reinsurance behavior also could be potentially driven by non-hedging motives, for example, capital and tax management (Tiller and Tiller 2009), it is unclear whether the shift in reinsurance is solely due to changes in the regulatory risk management incentives. To confirm the regulatory incentives hypothesis, I reestimate Equation (15) with the proportion of PRS liabilties reinsured as the dependent variable.Table 9(panel B) shows that the increase in PRS reinsurance after 2009 is correlated with the ex ante PRS ratio, that is, the increase is concentrated among insurers who do not hedge interest rate risk exposures with derivatives.

As changes in reinsurance activities also have been linked to financial constraints (Koijen and Yogo 2016), these regressions control for insurers’ financial position using a number of variables, including leverage, RBC ratio, credit rating, and BCAR ratio. I also control for insurers’ size using (log(assets)) to account for different abilities to engage in reinsurance. Overall, these findings show that insurers want to hedge PRS risks using ways other than derivatives. As a result, the composition of risk management for RS and PRS guarantees undergoes a shift after the regulation changed. RS exposures are increasingly hedged with derivatives, while PRS exposures are moved off-balance sheet using reinsurance.

5.1.3 Why do insurers not use even more reinsurance for PRS guarantees?

Two potential reasons could explain why insurers do not use even more reinsurance for PRS guarantees. First,Figure 7(panel B) shows that close to 75%of all reinsurance for VA goes to captives (shadow insurers), which are less regulated and often deemed as potential regulatory blind spots (OFR 2016). As a result, state regulators place limits on how much shadow insurance is permitted (Lawsky 2013). Second, third-party reinsurance can be costly (Froot and O’Connell 1999). These factors can limit the amount of PRS exposures that can be moved off-balance sheet using reinsurance.

5.2 Hedging with bonds

An alternative way to hedge the interest rate risk of VA guarantees is to buy a matching portfolio of bonds. To understand to what extent insurers hedge with bonds, I evaluate the following two hypotheses.Hypothesis 3: Insurers are already well hedged with bonds, which is why they do not hedge with derivatives prior to the shift in the regulation.Hypothesis 4: Insurers shift risk management from bonds to derivatives after the regulation changes but overall hedging remains the same.

Data and measurement:To evaluate these hypotheses, I exploit detailed holdings of all fixed income securities, including treasuries and government bonds, municipal bonds, agency and mortgage backed bonds, and corporate bonds, which insurers report at a CUSIP level in NAIC’s schedule D filings. The data are annual and span the period from 2004 to 2016. For each position, I observe the total par and market values, remaining maturity, coupon paid, and rating. To quantify the magnitude of interest rate risk hedging from bonds, one must account for both the size of the bond portfolio and the bonds’ maturities. To do so, I measure the interest rate exposures of bond holdings (bond exposures). Specifically, I compute the total dollar duration of bond holdings,δitB⁠, for each insureriat the end of each yeart⁠.31Dollar duration quantifies the total dollar change in the value of the bond holdings due to a 100-bps parallel shift in the yield curve.

5.2.1 Are insurers hedging VA guarantees with bonds?

To assess to what extent insurers use bonds to hedge the interest rate risk in VA guarantees, I regress the total dollar duration of bond holdings (bond exposures) on the various types of insurance liabilities: δ i t B ^ = β 1 G i t + β 2 L i f e i t + β 3 F A i t + β 4 I A i t + γ X i t + α t + α i + ϵ i t , where δ i t B ^ is the total dollar duration of bond holdings scaled by total capital for firm i at time t ⁠ . Independent variables G i t ⁠ , L i f e i t ⁠ , F A i t ⁠ , and I A i t denote VA guarantees, life, fixed annuities, and indexed annuities liabilities scaled by total capital, as described in Section 3.2 . The sample includes all insurers, including those that sell VA and those that do not.

Appendix Table C.5shows that the relationship between bond exposures and VA guarantees is not just statistically insignificant but also economically less important. To summarize the main findings, I find that (a) in the cross-section (column 1), higher bond exposures are associated with a higher Life, FA, and IA liabilities (⁠β2toβ4are positive and significant at the 1%level), butnothigher VA guarantees (⁠β1is insignificant). (b) The within-insurer variation (with insurer fixed effects) in bond exposures are alsoonlyrelated to non-VA liabilities (column 2). Thus, bond exposures comove with life, FA, and IA liabilities over time, but not with VA guarantees. The interpretation is that a dollar of life liabilities adds roughly 10 cents of dollar duration to the average insurer’s portfolio. In contrast a dollar of guarantees adds only 1.2 cents of dollar duration. (c) In columns 3 and 4, I exclusively focus on the preregulation period32and confirm that the same patterns hold true; that is, the cross-sectional and the within-insurer variation in bond exposures are related to non-VA liabilitiesonlyand not with VA guarantees.33Overall, these findings show that the evidence is not consistent with the hypothesis that insurers hedge VA guarantees with bonds in a meaningful way.

Next, I document the highly similar hedging patterns (with bonds) for insurers with and without VA guarantees. Motivated by the fact that bond exposures are primarily related to non-VA liabilities, I compare the bond exposures per unit of non-VA liabilities for insures with and without VA guarantees, that is, for insurerswith VA, I only scale bond exposures by the non-VA portion of their liabilities.Appendix Table C.6shows that for every dollar of non-VA liabilities, both the groups on average have roughly 10 cents of dollar duration from bonds. A test of difference in means reveals no significant differences, implying that both the groups hedge non-VA liabilities in a similar way with bonds. Moreover, bond exposures (per unit of non-VA liabilities) are also highly stable for both groups over time. This suggests that insurers adjust bond exposures as non-VA liabilities change, consistent with the evidence inAppendix Table C.5that insurers hedge non-VA liabilities with bonds.34

5.2.2 Is there substitution from bonds to derivatives?

Next, I evaluate whether insurers shift out of bonds and into derivatives once the regulation changes. To do so, I examine how bond exposures have changed after the shift in the regulation for insurers with different exposures to RS and PRS guarantees by estimating the specification in Equation (15) with the dependent variable as bond exposures,δitB^⁠. If insurers exposed to RS guarantees, for whom hedging with derivatives increased substantially, substituted from bonds and into derivatives once the regulation changed then we expectβRSto be negative. Moreover, if overall hedging remained the same, we expect no change in thetotal hedgingexposures, which include both derivatives and bonds.

Appendix Table C.7negates these predictions. First, columns 1-3 show estimations on bond exposures only.βRSis statistically indistinguishable from zero. In particular, there is no evidence of a decrease in bond exposures after the regulation changes for high RS insurers. Second, columns 4-6 show estimations on bond and derivatives exposures jointly.βRSis positive and significant; that is, there is an increase intotal hedgingfor high RS insurers and the increase in hedging with derivatives is not cancelled out by a simultaneous decrease in hedging with bonds. Thus, the evidence is not consistent with the hypothesis that insurers substitute out of bonds and into derivatives and that overall hedging remains the same. Moreover, there is no meaningful change in hedging for high PRS insurers with bonds (⁠βPRSis insignificant in column 1). Combined with the result that bond exposures are not correlated with VA guarantees, this implies that insurers highly exposed to PRS guarantees do not hedge with bonds despite the lack of hedging with derivatives.

5.2.3 Why do insurers not hedge VA guarantees with bonds?

Three reasons could potentially explain why insurers do not hedge VA guarantees with bonds.35First, VA guarantees have complex payoffs and risk exposures. As a result, they are easier to hedge using derivatives than bonds.36Second, hedging VA guarantees requires frequent rebalancing37(also seeTable 6), and it may be more difficult to rebalance bonds without price impact. Indeed, insurers’ bond holdings are mostly corporate bonds and tend to be illiquid. Thus, trading gives rise to large price impact (Bretscher et al. 2020;Ellul et al. 2020;Sen and Sharma 2020). Moreover, gains and losses from trading in bonds affect capital in the same way as MTM changes in the value of derivatives. Third, because of the cash flow structure of VAs, swaps are preferred because they provide the same exposure as bonds but require limited upfront payment.38
article.6 Quantifying and Interpreting the Risk:
Overall, this paper shows that insurers hedge the specific risks that the regulatory regime starts recognizing. An immediate implication of my findings is that because the regulatory regime does not yet recognize interest rate risk for PRS guarantees, interest rate mismatch still remains. In this section, I quantify the economic significance of the regulatory changes and their impact on insurers’ hedging and risk exposures. I also discuss interpretations of the risk management shifts and how the new regime interacts with interest rates in a way that can further exacerbate risk mismatch going forward.

6.1 Quantifying overall hedging and insurers’ interest rate exposures

I start by quantifying hedging for the insurance sector in the aggregate incorporating all risk management tools. To determine the fraction of VA exposures hedged, I need three main inputs for each guarantee: (1) estimates of gross risk exposures before hedging, as risk exposures are not directly reported; (2) the fraction of liabilities hedged using alternative ways (reinsurance). I ignore bonds because insurers do not hedge VA with bonds (see Section5.2); and (3) estimates of offsetting exposures from derivatives due to the shift in the regulation.Table 10documents the key ingredients.
