6.1.1 Interest rate hedging and risk exposures

To quantify the magnitude of hedging, I compute the ratio of exposures from derivatives (panel E) to the exposures of the guarantees net of reinsurance (panel D). Derivatives hedge close to 77%of the net interest rate exposures for RS guarantees, compared to only 5%for PRS, after the regulation shifts (panel F). Thus, while the regulation meaningfully impacts exposures of insurers with RS guarantees, insurers with PRS guarantees still have a large mismatch. Finally, panel G reports total hedging, which is the ratio of offsetting exposures (from both derivatives and reinsurance) to gross exposures. After the regulatory changes, the hedging gap between RS and PRS increase substantially: insurers hedge over 80%of RS exposures, the bulk of which takes place through derivatives. However, a large fraction of PRS exposures (⁠∼60%⁠) remain unhedged.

More broadly, the remaining interest rate exposures of VA guarantees (after accounting for reinsurance and derivatives) is substantial. For example, in 2012, the exposures equal $28.4B (9.5%of total capital). This suggests that a 100-bps decline in rates would lead to a 9.5%decline in regulatory capital for the insurance sector in the aggregate. Moreover, bulk of the exposures arise from PRS guarantees. This is because PRS constitute a large share (two-thirds) of VA liabilities (panel A) and the hedging gap between RS and PRS is large.

These estimates are consistent with the large sensitivities of insurers’ stock returns to interest rate changes.Appendix Table C.9measures the sensitivities of stock returns to the equity-market and 10-year Treasury returns. The loading on 10-year Treasury returns is high for large VA underwriters. For example, insurers in the high (above-median) VA group have a sensitivity of−1.2. This means that the stock returns’ sensitivity of large VA underwriters is close to the sensitivity of a 10-year bond, which has a duration of about 9.1 years. A coefficient of−1.2, thus, can be interpreted as a duration of∼11 years (1.2×9.1). In other words, a 100-bps decline in rates would cause the average insurer’s equity in this group to decrease by 11%⁠, which is similar in magnitude to the estimates obtained inTable 10.41Appendix Ddiscusses additional robustness for the estimates presented in this section.

6.2 Interpreting the risk management shifts

One could interpret the empirical results in two possible ways depending on insurers’ baseline hedging motives.42One interpretation is that prior to the regulatory changes insurers wanted to hedge more, but regulation disincentivized hedging by not properly recognizing risks (Koijen and Yogo 2022;Credit Suisse 2012). Prior to 2009, liabilities were largely insensitive, while derivatives were marked-to-market. As a result, if insurers had tried to properly hedge their economic risks under the old regulatory regime, that would have generally increased the volatility of their regulatory capital. To examine whether this is the case, I recompute insurers’ regulatory capital (prior to 2009) assuming their derivatives usage was more commensurate with their economic exposures.Appendix Figure C.1shows that had insurers hedged economic exposures prior to 2009 in the same way as they did after the regulatory changes, the volatility of regulatory capitalwithderivatives would have been substantially higher than the volatility of regulatory capitalwithoutderivatives.43Since the volatility of regulatory capital can be costly, it can then create incentives for insurers to not hedge.

An alternative interpretation is that insurers did not want to hedge, for example, because of risk shifting motives due to the existence of government guarantees (Lee, Mayers, and Smith 1997). The new regime - by recognizing risks - simply forced insurers to hedge some VA guarantees more. The evidence on reinsurance, discussed in Section5, however, is consistent with the view that insurers want to hedge their economic risks. We observe insurers employing more reinsurance for PRS relative to RS after 2009, which suggests that they want to hedge PRS even though the regulatory regime does not fully incentivize hedging of PRS risks. This suggests that the introduction of the new regime helped restore insurers’ hedging incentives, but only for a subset of VA liabilities as the regulation does not yet recognize interest rate risk for PRS guarantees.

6.3 Monetary policy and financial fragility

An important aspect of the new regime is that it can stop recognizing interest rate risk altogether when rates increase. When interest rates are low, the Stochastic framework dominates with a higher likelihood because the value of the embedded guarantees rise. As interest rates increase, the value of Stochastic framework liabilities reduce and could fall well below Standard framework liabilities, which are fixed and do not change as interest rates change. Using surveys, Junus and Motiwalla (2010) estimate that the Standard framework dominated for a large number of VA underwriters when the regulation was first introduced. As interest rates fell, the Stochastic framework started to dominate. Thus, going forward, as interest rates start to rise, the Standard framework may start to dominate once again and the incentives to hedge could switch off even for guarantees that are currently risk sensitive.

InAppendix Table C.10, I find suggestive evidence that the regulatory framework interacts with interest rates to affect hedging incentives. The threshold rate above which the Standard framework binds varies in the cross-section. This is because the valuation liabilities under the Standard framework depends on the valuation rate (the prevailing rate at the time a contract is originated). If insurers underwrote when rates were low (high), they would have a lower (higher) valuation rate and a higher (lower) Standard framework liabilities. This would imply a higher (lower) propensity to have the Standard framework binding. Using sales data from Morningstar Annuity to identify insurers for whom the Standard framework is more likely to bind, inAppendix Table C.10, I find that insurers that have underwritten in years when rates were low (and thus have a higher likelihood of the Standard framework binding) have a greater likelihood to shut down hedging when rates rise. This implies that when interest rates eventually rise, incentives to hedge could switch off completely. This can further exacerbate insurers’ risk mismatch going forward.
article.7 Conclusion:
Despite large and rapid shifts in risk regulation since the financial crisis, little systematic evidence exists on the ways in which hedging decisions of financial institutions are shaped by the regulatory framework they operate under. In this paper, I quantify the hedging incentives of U.S. life insurers who sell investment products containing long-dated minimum return guarantees called Variable Annuities. I measure the risk exposures of insurers’ derivatives portfolio over a long period of time and across risk factors. I show that VA guarantees with similar economic risks are treated differently by regulation and these differences impact insurers’ hedging behavior. When the regulatory regime recognizes certain risks, insurers start to hedge these risks in a substantial way. For some guarantees, this involves hedging both interest rate and equity risks, but, for others, it involves hedging only equity risk. Because the regulatory regime does not yet recognize interest rate risk for several VA guarantees, insurers remain exposed to substantial interest rate risk. My findings have implications for the fragility of the insurance sector, which is exacerbated by shifts in interest rates as regulatory incentives to hedge shut down when interest rates rise.

There are several reasons to regulate life insurers. Regulation aims to mitigate the moral hazard and the excessive risk-taking that arises because insurers finance themselves too cheaply due to the presence of state guaranty funds (Bohn and Hall 1999) and implicit bailout guarantees (e.g., AIG). Households lack the ability to properly monitor the risks and long-term solvency of insurers, but depend on insurance products to smooth the idiosyncratic and aggregate risks they face. Fragility of insurers could affect households’ propensity to buy insurance, which could result in increases in precautionary savings and welfare loss for households. Life insurers are also among the largest investors in many fixed income markets and a source of long-term capital for corporations, municipalities, and other agencies. Fragility of insurers could give rise to fire sale externalities and increases in borrowing costs, which could have contractionary consequences for the economy.

While the rationale for having regulation is clear, developing appropriate standards to adequately measure the risks of financial products is challenging. In particular, there are several reasons the regulatory framework governing VAs could have inconsistencies. VAs are relatively new products, gaining prominence only since the 1990s. VAs also have more complex payoffs and are hedged using different instruments relative to traditional insurance products. My setting shows that regulators, when faced with the full complexity of financial products, could have a difficult time getting the treatment of the risks exactly right, and more so across products, risk factors, and under different market conditions.44

More broadly, the types of regulatory incentives I identify are prevalent in other parts of the financial sector. For example, the regulatory framework governing insurance companies in Europe, known as Solvency II, has important deviations from market values. Differences also exist in the risk sensitivities and the treatment of derivatives in the model-based versus the standardized frameworks under Basel III. The findings of my paper could, therefore, extend to banks and insurers globally.

35 This subsection is developed based on a telephone interview with Ken Mungan (Chairman) and Peter Sun (Principal and Consulting Actuary) at Milliman (August 4, 2021). I refer to this interview as Milliman (2021) throughout the paper.

36 That investors prefer derivatives to hedge more complex risks is true in other contexts. For example, U.S. investors exposed to interest rate risks in mortgage-backed securities have largely relied on interest rate swaps rather than treasuries for dynamic hedging ( Perli and Sack 2003 ; Reinhart and Sack (2000) ).

37 Underlying risk exposures change frequently with changes in both interest rates and equity market. When equity markets do well (and guarantees become out-of-the-money), interest rate exposures would be small. A sudden decline in the equity market, however, could give rise to a large increase in exposures.

38 Swaps are levered positions on the underlying bond. The MTM value of swaps is lower than that of bonds (of similar exposure) as the fixed and the floating legs offset each other. Klingler and Sundaresan (2019) argue that underfunded pension funds use swaps instead of bonds to match long-dated liabilities. Domanski, Shin, and Sushko (2017) make the same argument in the context of guaranteed insurance liabilities in Europe.

39 I am assuming that the account value and the notional value are the same, which would be true if the guarantees are at-the-money. In robustness, I allow the contract’s moneyness to vary to check the sensitivity of my results to this assumption.

40 This is because of negligible exposures in the base year (2004), as Figure 4 shows, and the coefficients in Figure 5 for 2007 are insignificant.

41 It is worth noting that stock returns’ sensitivities may not reflect the underlying risk exposures. (a) There may be limited disclosure of risk exposures. (b) Parent companies’ sensitivities are also affected by the interest rate exposures of other subsidiaries. (c) Sensitivities likely also capture investors’ perceptions about the sensitivity of future demand for insurance to interest rates and not just current exposures.

42 It is not necessary to know the the baseline hedging motives for the purpose of identifying the effects of the regulatory changes – under either interpretation, the design of the regulatory framework affects insurers’ hedging decisions. However, it is useful for interpreting the empirical facts.

43 Note that the relevant metric is not the volatility of regulatory capital with the actual amount of derivatives insurers used. But it is the volatility of regulatory capital in a (counterfactual) world where the amount of derivatives insurers used would properly hedge the economic risks. This is because in the old regulatory regime, the risks of liabilities were recognized in a few states of the world. For example, to some extent, equity market movements were recognized during extreme downturns (see Section 1 ). As a result, in such instances some amount of derivatives could have been possibly used, and these derivatives would then reduce the volatility of capital (e.g., in 2008). In fact, Appendix Figure C.1 confirms that insurers are more likely to use derivatives when doing so did not increase the volatility of regulatory capital.

44 There have been growing efforts to identify the weaknesses in the statutory framework and steps required to properly align the regulatory risk sensitivities with economic exposures ( Oliver Wyman 2018 ).
